kills the stealth Ward how you can still see her as she walks away Vision in the area will last for a much longer time than most people realize as it's a whole 5 Seconds despite the ward being removed titre: HOW TO PLAY the NEW League of Legends TCG! | Riftbound Tutorial
transcription: Hey summoners, welcome to Riftbound, the League of Legends trading card game where champions clash, spells fly, and battlefields decide who comes out on top. If you're brand new, don't worry. I'm going to walk you through everything you need to set up and play your very first game step by step, crystal clear. Let's get into it. So, here's what you need. Each player brings a main deck with at least 40 cards filled with units, spells, and gear. a rune deck of exactly 12 rune cards. These power your plays. A champion legend, which is the face of your deck, and a few battlefield cards where the fight happens. Your main deck must match your champion's domain, like fury, chaos, order. They're usually different colors, and you're allowed up to three copies of any individual card, and three signature cards, the unique abilities tied to your champion. Now, here's how you set up a game. First, you place your champion legend face up, public for both players. Then, you put your chosen champion, which is a unit with the same name as your champion legend that you've picked out, in your champion zone. Then, you shuffle your main deck and rune deck into separate piles. I'd recommend giving them different sleeve colors. Then each player brings three battlefields each. And then you shuffle those three battlefields, randomly pick two to play with, one from each player, and then you lay them between you. To decide who goes first, you either roll a dieice or flip a coin or whatever you and the other player agree on is fair. Then finally, you each draw four cards. A quick note on mulligans. You can mulligan a card in your hand if you don't like it. Let's say you have four cards in hand and you don't like a certain one. What you can do is you can pick that card out, put it at the bottom of your deck, and then draw a new one. You can do this for up to two cards when you draw your first hand. And don't shuffle. Now, on to the turns. We follow a very simple A B C D structure for the start of every turn, followed by all the other actions you can take afterwards. It's as simple as that. First is a the awaken phase. This is where you stand up. You ready all your exhausted cards, your units, your gears, and your runes. Anything that was exhausted in the turn before is now unexhausted or awakened. B is the beginning phase. Effects and points go here. You trigger any start of turn effects, usually cards, specify beginning phase here, and you score one point for each battlefield you control. And then C, the channel phase. This is where your draw power comes along. You draw two rune cards from your rune deck and place them face up in your rune area. I'd recommend stacking the same kinds of runes on top of each other. These are what you'll use to pay for cards this turn. And runes stay on the board unless you use them to pay for something. What you draw is what you've got for this round. And D, the draw phase, one more card, where you draw one card from your main deck. Simple as that. And that's your hand refreshed. And now it's go time. What happens from here is you can either pass your turn if you do so please or you can have some fun. This is where the action starts. The action phase, the showdown phase, the combat phase, all galore. You can choose to take any unexhausted units being units that are awakened and put them onto battlefields to start combat. You can use spells if you'd like and you can use any effects that your cards allow you to as long as you have the cost that you need, the runes that you need. And once you finished your turn, you can then pass your turn to your opponent. Now, let's cover a quick combat example. Let's go Vi versus Victor's minions. Let's say you have Vi with three might defending a battlefield. Your opponent sends in three one might minions led by Victor. A total of three might on their side, one each. At this moment, the battlefield is contested and a showdown begins. You've got a spell in hand, Rebuke. It costs two runes and you've got the runes for it. So, you exhaust them. You recycle them to the bottom of your rune deck to pay for the cost and cast Rebuke. Rebuke returns one enemy unit on the battlefield to its owner's hand. You bounce one of Victor's minions back to his hand and now the opponent only has two might across two minions and you still got Vi at three. Combat begins. Vi deals three damage, enough to knock out both minions. She takes two in return, but she's still standing. You win the fight. You control the battlefield and you're up one point. Simple as. At the end of your turn, remember that all temporary buffs expire, units heal, so you remove all damage. Everything resets for the next turn. Then you pass to your opponent. But one final note, to score that last winning point once you're at seven, you need one more to get to eight. Well, this is important. You can only score your final point, your eighth point, in two ways. Let's bring it back to the battlefield for a quick example. You're sitting at seven points and you want the final point to win the game. In this scenario, Victor's minions are out again. They're swarming the field and you've got Jinx pushing into one battlefield and Vi holding her ground on another. Here's how you seal the win. You can either option one, conquer both battlefields on your turn by moving units and winning showdowns. In this case, Jinx pushes through and Vi holds a line and boom, you've controlled both battlefields and now you've scored your eighth point. Or you can go with option two. you're already holding a battlefield on the other side and your opponent doesn't contest it or fails to contest it with whatever you can prevent. And this means that on your next turn, as long as you stay holding that battlefield, as long as Vi still standing strong when your turn begins and she's still in control, you score that final eighth point. In either case, once you begin your turn with at least one battlefield under your control and you're already at seven points, you score the final point and win the game. So, when you're at seven, play smart, time your push, or dig in and hold. That's the final piece. If you need info on how to play other modes, like a 2v2, a free-for-all, or whatever else Riot may bring along, let us know. Any other questions, and maybe you want to learn some more about some cards, let us know in the comments, and we'll see what we can do. But for now, grab your decks, outsmart your rivals, and I'll see you on the rift summoners.

titre: How to Play Like an Ahri MAIN! - ULTIMATE AHRI GUIDE
transcription: ari is one of the most popular mid lane champions in league of legends and it's not hard to understand why she's got insane burst incredible cc and is super mobile all of these strengths allow her to be played in many different ways which inherently makes her more appealing to a wider audience not only that but ari is one of those champions that are for the most part fairly simple to play but also have a ton of outplay potential in her kit at the same time this means that you can pick up the champion and start carrying quickly but there's a ton of room in her kit to start making montage plays and hard carrying games in today's guide we're going to teach you how to play like an ari main right from the start going from the basics all the way to the things that most players don't know that you can do on ari which make all the difference we will kick this off with ahri's playstyle into power spikes combos tips and tricks runes build and skill order jumping straight into her playstyle when people think of ahri they often think of a hyper mobile assassin picking off enemy carries throughout the map and in team fights and this is one way to play ari but certainly not the only way ari's diversity allows her to carry the game in many different ways in fact one of the best ways to play her is to actually fight alongside your team you don't necessarily need to be fancy to have high impact as ari standing by your teammates and landing charms on vital targets is one of the more effective ways to play her the major reason why this backline style is so effective is because of her charm charms are one of if not the strongest forms of cc in the game and only ari evelyn and rakan have access to it let's consider the point of cc in general obviously the idea is to lock down your opponents so that you and your team can damage them for free the beauty of charm is that it works as a stun but it's also pulling the opponent even further out of position from their team not only are they unable to do anything throughout the charm's duration but when they come out of it they'll be even further out of position resulting in more damage taken outside of team fighting ari has a couple of alternate playstyles in which she can play out a game one is looking for picks once the mid game comes around make sure that you swap to a sweeper and always have a control ward on hand a ton of ari's damage comes from landing charm setting up in fog of war is a reliable way of landing it on unsuspecting opponents face checking into you she's also fairly reliable at split pushing when ahead she can beat out a ton of split pushing champions with her mobility and even if she sometimes has some questionable moments her ultimate enables her to get out of sticky situations sometimes even picking up a kill on her way out alright jumping into ari's power spikes the first one you should know is at level three if you put two points into her q it allows her to clear the back caster creeps with two cues this gives ari some of the strongest early game wave clear you can abuse this with a very consistent strategy fast push the fourth minion wave with two cues then you can take a free recall and you can come back to lane with full health and mana along with an item advantage this is a great way to get guaranteed leads over your opponents her second big power spike comes at level 6. ari's biggest problem until then is landing charm reliably against opponents who play well to avoid it ari gains 20 extra damage on her spells when she lands her charm which is why her ultimate is such a huge power spike it gives her new ways to reposition in order to land it lastly ahri's third noteworthy power spike comes at around level 10 where you'll be often completing your glp any first item is a power spike but glp is unique for ari as it allows her to more consistently land her charm landing it is critical for her success so once you have this item it makes scoring kills ridiculously easy okay let's jump into some of the combos that you need to know as ari first up is the famous and all-important charm flash the execution of this is really simple it's literally the name of the combo instead of charming after you flash you press it before instead by doing it in this order it cancels your charm's animation making it come out faster this is a great way to find angles around obstacles preventing you from landing charm your charm will always fly in the direction where your mouse was before you flashed basically if your mouse was initially here you press e then flash here at the end of it all your charm will always go where your mouse was the second that you pressed e this is a fantastic combo to know specifically during the lane phase ari has a hard time landing her charm through minions but you can charm flash to angle the charm past enemy minions for easy kills next up is her charm ultimate this particular combo is used for when you want to hide your charm's animation during an engage press charm first and then r you'll travel along with your charm hiding it from view potentially catching your opponents off guard now we'll start to get fancy take those two previous combos and smack them together for the ultimate charm flash press charm then flash then your ultimate the point just like before is to animation cancel your charm so it comes out faster while also hiding it from your opponents with your ultimate animation this combo is highly effective but if you notice there's a tiny problem with it often times when you use it you'll end up very close to your opponents so after you charm they'll be right on top of you throwing q here means that it'll take a while for the return damage this usually won't matter but it may give certain champions time to get out of your charm and avoid the true damage part of your q on the way back so we take the previous combo another step further for your surprise burst combo we know by now that you can animation cancel with charm flash you can also do it with q as well in the same way ari is one of the unique champions in the game where you can animation cancel two abilities at the same time with flash get ready you need fast fingers for this one press e and q then flash then your ultimate doing it this way makes it so that your q flies out before your ultimate guaranteeing that both parts of the damage go off this combo is especially useful versus champions that you don't want to give a single moment to recover like echo with his arm alright now we'll dive into the tips and tricks that only remains know to make your life a lot easier first up is canceling dashes riot removed this a while back since it was so powerful but gave it back to ari when she wasn't performing too well if you time your charm correctly then you cancel enemy dashes leaps and jumps midway through this interaction makes the ari leblanc matchup famous in particular if ari reacts quickly enough to charm her w it actually cancels out the damage entirely often winning ari the lane outright next keep in mind that arya's w orbs are heat seeking missiles that orbit around her while they may have the same range as her if they're behind her they won't fire until they themselves are in range of the target try to keep this in mind whenever you're looking to burst someone while ari can maintain her distance you may want to get up close and personal to fire off all your w orbs immediately onto your opponent also keep in mind that the orbs prioritize ari's charm target or the last target that she auto-attacked to make sure she gets full value always make sure that you auto-attack as you press w so there's no damage wasted on minions during the laning phase next up is a small tip for landing both parts of your queue keep in mind that your queue will always go back to you not where you threw it from one of the main uses of ari's ultimate is being able to reposition to guarantee that her return damage lands doing so is incredibly easy once you throw out your q there's a really big delay as it turns around use this time to think about where you'll want to reposition towards so that your lane opponent is in the middle setting up the return true damage much more consistently if you incorporate your flash into this you can get some q return angles that would never be possible with just your ultimate during the laning phase though you usually don't have access to your ultimate and landing charm is very inconsistent with so many minions in the way with q being your main form of harass you'll need to find ways to ensure that you land both parts of the damage a very simple trick for this is to position at max range from your opponents in lane then look for opportunities to throw out your q since it's max range if it lands then the outgoing and return damage go off at the same time this is an all or nothing approach to landing your q as ari if they dodge the initial q you'll miss all the damage but if not you'll land all of it this is a great method to use when your opponent will be predictably stationary such as when they're going for a last hit as for your q's passive you're definitely going to want to optimize how much you heal from it always try to use it on a full wave so that it hits both going out and coming back in for a total of 12 ticks of healing or 14 during a cannon weight during the laning phase when you might be scared to walk up to the wave you can always chuck a cue at the raptor camp for some free healing as the lane progresses this heal will really start to power up as you get some ap there's even a little trick here to maximize your healing line up your cue like this throw it out then walk a bit to the right then back to the left this just ensures that you proc your q as much as possible on the chickens our final tip is an important one for ari's ultimate there's often times where you won't need all of your ultimate charges for a kill if you know for certain that you don't need them anymore then you should use your remaining charges as quickly as possible this is because your ultimate only goes on cooldown after you've used all charges by sitting on charges you'll have to wait longer for it to come off of cooldown alright let's finish this off with what you'll want to build on ari for your skill order go q e q w for your first four levels then prioritize maxine r then q then w and finally e as four items there's two different builds that you can run on re the glacial path or the burst path you'll want to run the glacial build whenever you see a lot of immobile squishy champions on the enemy team along with your glp you'll have a devastating slow making your charm a breeze allowing you to pick up a ton of easy kills for the glacial path build brush glp as your first item afterwards build twin shadows for more slowing capabilities followed by the leandre's torment which synergizes with all the slows you'll have then just finish off your build with situational ap items based on what you're fighting against as for the runes you'll run with this build just go a standard glacial augment build make sure that you run the item cdr rune in the domination tree it's critical to run this rune so you can spam out your glp as often as possible with this build we don't really recommend running that build when the enemy team has a lot of mobility tools though slows are less effective against champions with blinks dashes etc in those cases you'll want to run a more burst electrocute page to maximize your burst potential so you may have noticed our sweet spirit blossom design for today's guide that's all thanks to our collaborations we've been doing with our friends at titre: How Challengers Control Mouse Movement in League of Legends!

character's hitbox actually is if you aim for the body or head you'll often miss your skill shot when you think that you shouldn't have so we got our skillcap.com subscribers to submit point of view footage of them playing in order to analyze exactly what they're doing wrong with their mouse this was a massive project and let me tell you the results were shocking in that whether they were bronze gold or diamond players were often making the exact same fatal flaws and the first mistake we saw all players making is that they were spamming right clicks on enemy champions in team fights watch this clip of a bronze jinx they're in a team fight turn on the enemy xayah and for five seconds straight they spam their right click on top of her this is a big mistake because between each auto attack or spellcast you should be sending a movement command to make you both harder to hit as well as chase down our kite more effectively we'll get into this in more detail shortly but i know you're thinking well she's bronze and i'm not i don't do this so it doesn't apply to me well check out this silver player on zen sao right after he engages watch his mouse he's spamming right clicks on the enemy champion never moving in between auto attacks still too low elo for you to be convinced okay how about this platinum player on kane he spams his right click on the enemy center when chasing and when she entered wraith form she became camouflage and so he ended up just standing still awkwardly because of this mistake the point is most players are doing this at all ranks without even knowing that they're doing it so what should you be doing instead well you need to develop the habit of always and i really do mean always inputting a movement command after an attack command you should never be standing still just autoing or only spamming the attack command when chasing arc kiting why is this such a big problem well firstly you're going to be way less effective at kiting and chasing people down which will result in lowering your damage per minute for example watch this bronze trinomir look to take the scuttle he's only right clicking on the scuttle instead of moving in between auto attacks here's a challenger player on tryndamere with the exact same items and level and by moving in between auto attacks you're able to cover more distance and spend less time chasing and more time auto attacking increasing your damage per second so some players may already know this but they don't understand exactly how this is possible it's much simpler once you begin to understand how auto attack animations work in league of legends there's essentially two parts to your attack animation the first is the wind-up you never want to move during this first part of the animation or you'll cancel the auto attack then there's the second part of the animation that occurs after your projectile releases or you damage something in melee form by inputting a movement command immediately after this first half of the auto attack animation you can actually cancel the second half of the animation this is where you essentially save time and what lets you move more between auto attacks by being able to move more while attacking the same amount of times you can either kite better or chase down players better effectively increasing your damage per second for example let's check out skill cap's challenger expert 6x playing sivir in a smurf commentary on our website the enemy team starts diving him so he kites back following that rhythm of swapping between an auto attack and a movement command over and over never standing still most players understand this concept but where they get stuck is when it's not clear if they should be cutting backwards or forwards for example jumping back to that jinx clip and you can see why she was spamming right click on the enemy xayah she wasn't sure whether she should be moving forward or backwards so she just kind of stands there when in this situation you always want to be moving sideways in between auto attacks the reason you want to do this is that it makes you much harder to hit with any projectiles to jump back to our challenger expert six axe check out what he does in a similar situation when he's auto attacking the set he's at max auto attack range so he doesn't need to kite back he doesn't need to move forward since set isn't running away and so he defaults into side to side erratic movement by the way when we say that you should be doing this all the time we really do mean all the time watch any challenger or pro player even when they're auto attacking minions in lane and they follow this role of moving in between each auto attack there's actually a really effective way to train this mechanic by jumping into the practice mode select an 80 carry and when you get into the game make yourself level 18 and buy yourself attack speed boots and an attack speed item the reason we do this is that having more attack speed and movement speed will generally make it a bit more forgiving and easier to practice disable minions from spawning and place a target dummy in mid lane at first you can start with simple drills try just walking forward always following that pattern of one auto attack then one movement command then you can try it backwards then try to the left and then to the right once you get the hang of the basics you can place additional target dummies to move further and try more complicated patterns such as for every three attacks for do one attack backwards you can then challenge yourself even further by making a quicker pattern of two attacks forward and one back you can then swap between them to again increase the difficulty this is very similar to traditional training techniques that a boxer might use to drain their timing and rhythm of their punches and dodges in short you really should never be clicking more than once on an enemy champion develop the habit of auto attacking once and then inputting a movement command and repeating that pattern in pretty much every situation moving on the second mistake we noticed all players making is they consistently click way too far from their champion let me show you what i mean here's a silver player i want you to pay attention to their mouse while they're moving in lane at first this probably doesn't seem like anything out of the ordinary or that they really aren't doing anything noticeably wrong now here's a challenger player again pay attention to their mouse this time though i want you to take note of how close they're clicking to their champion they're always sending movement commands very close to their character great with this knowledge in the back of your mind let's go back to that silver player it should be painfully obvious now what they're doing wrong they send movement commands extremely far away from their champion compared to the challenger so why is this such a big problem well it severely lowers your reaction time if i click far away from my champion to the left and then suddenly need to dodge to the right my mouse has to move across a lot more space this extra distance it travels takes more time it also increases issues of inaccuracy compare this to if i'm clicking very close to my champion there's less distance for my mouse to travel if i need to suddenly change directions this allows me to react and change directions faster while also making me more accurate a lot of players aren't even aware of this idea when you're moving try to visualize a small circle around your champion about this big after analyzing pros rarely do they exceed past the circle keep your mouse within the circle when inputting movement commands you only move your mouse past this circle in order to input attack commands fire skill shots or to pan your camera and this perfectly transitions into our next mistake players make they either click way too much or way too little here's a bronze tryndamere watches clicks and you'll notice two things first as you just learned it's now painfully obvious he's clicking too far away from his champion you're welcome you'll never not be able to see that from now on secondly his clicking frequency is far too low he's simply not clicking enough times per second compare this to our challenger expert evnis notice how many more clicks per second that he's going to input so why is this important let's simplify it at first to really understand the concept let's say i have to move to my left and i'm only allowed four mouse clicks due to the game's auto passing it makes my movement very smooth and i change directions only three times this smoothness combined with very few direction changes makes you easy to predict and land skill shots on now compare this to if i'm allowed to click as many times as i want it almost looks like i'm jittery and much harder to predict additionally i'm inputting more clicks per second so i can quickly react and change directions if you watch someone with a low amount of clicks in lane not only are they easier to hit with skill shots it's also just easier to read when they're trying to go aggressive or what direction they plan to move compare this to a challenger player and you'll notice it's very hard to get a read on them they feel very jittery and it's hard to predict whether they're trying to go aggressive or what direction they want to move in now before you get ahead of yourself and just start clicking like a madman you can actually go too far in the other direction to drive home this point let's take a look at the pro player chovi he's considered to be one of the best players in the world when it comes to mouse movement i want you to watch this insane play he makes in real time and see press q then over karma to press q it's here he doesn't even send another command since his champion will automatically attack karma once his q finishes instead he's already hovered his cursor over his next target set in the meantime his champion auto attacks after the auto attack finishes he then presses q on the set he then hovers to the next minion and presses q even though it seemed like he would be clicking like crazy in reality he only moved his mouse five times and in a very controlled smooth fashion now let's compare this to a diamond player just like with chovi let's watch this clip first in real time alright that wasn't amazing but didn't exactly seem that bad either here let's slow it down now there's going to be some issues that you'll notice like he's inputting movement commands far away from his champion but that's not what we're going to be focusing on instead let's freeze frame on when he's being inefficient with his mouse alright his mouse is currently in the middle of nowhere for no apparent reason did you catch that let's rewind his mouse just went beside him but he didn't actually input a movement command and he just did some insane windmill technique drawing half a circle on his screen keep in mind this is a diamond player and to be honest he had some of the better mechanics compared to the other diamond players that we reviewed the point is players are often like the tryndamere not clicking enough and so they are slow and easy to read or they have decent clicking frequency or maybe click too much given their accuracy and they become highly inefficient with their movement to the point that they become inaccurate just to be clear always value accuracy first and then try to push yourself to click 10 to 20 more frequently once that frequency becomes the new normal for you and you're just as accurate as before then increase the frequency by 10 to 20 again it's important to build good accuracy habits first and then introduce a gradual increase in speed that's the key to getting challenger level mouse movement alright we really did save the best for last as one of the worst mistakes player make has to do with how they pan their cameras while fighting for example let's take a look at the silver jinx she's chasing down the enemy 80 carry but is unable to pan their camera up while also managing their attack moving forward suddenly we've lost the zey's health bar and don't know if we should flash otto to kill her what a lot of lower elo players do is they use the center on champion command default bound to spacebar and is kind of like a crutch for this issue for example here's a diamond sejuani watch how she doesn't once use her mouse to pan the camera during this fight instead to prevent wukong from getting out of vision she holds spacebar to keep the camera centered on herself this does seem harmless at first and possibly even like a good solution and it can be in the right situation however it can also severely limit you here's a different diamond player on kog'maw a fight breaks out and they fall back on holding spacebar so they don't know how to pan their camera effectively with their mouse we now can't see the enemies health bars or how close our riven is to engaging we completely lack information the fight continues while they hold spacebar if we pause we can barely see the enemy set above us meanwhile half our screen is filled with the terrain below us which contains no enemies or any valuable information freeze a little bit later and suddenly a pike just pops into frame if our camera was panned upward slightly we could have seen him much earlier and again jyn semi disappears off frame and we can't see his health bar so to understand what you should be doing let's take a look at a little known pro player you might have heard of him his name's faker to start this clip his entire camera is already panned below him he's literally on the top of his screen and it gives him so much more information on what's happening in front of him as he moves forward he nudges his camera back slightly so we can see his flank a bit better to his right his team is winning the fight the enemy is carrying away so he then pans his camera with his mouse a bit more forward as he chases now he's being turned on you can see he nudges this camera up a bit since he knows he's probably going to have to kite back and so needs to see a bit more behind him as he kites back he continues to nudge his camera back slightly this is allowing him to have his camera still lean a bit more to the left where the enemies are to get maximum information just watch how the enemy galio starts to disappear below our camera faker immediately nudges the camera down to get a better view then as soon as they turn the fight back onto the enemy he starts nudging his camera in the direction he's moving always keeping the camera slightly ahead of his champion this is how the best players team fight so well they're able to see the maximum amount of information on what the enemy team is doing by panning their camera manually the majority of the time so what does all this mean for you well as we saw earlier in the low elo replays you're probably holding spacebar way too often and not manually panning your camera enough and what we found out is that a big reason for this is that most players actually don't know how to execute this mechanically so remember earlier when we said when inputting movement commands you always want to click close to your champion well panning your camera is the exception to this rule to pan your camera properly you want to do a quick nudge on the border of your screen this will force you to send movement commands a bit further out than usual this is totally normal there's actually a great way to train this in practice mode first just try getting the hang of nudging your camera so that your champion is in view at the bottom of your screen you can just hit spacebar to reset and keep training yourself to nudge your camera just enough in that sweet spot where your champion is at the edge of your screen now if you are nudging the top of your screen and your champion repeatedly disappears below your camera you may want to try decreasing camera move speed in your options the inverse would be if you nudge the camera and it barely moves up at all you may want to increase the camera move speed you want to keep practicing this until you're able to consistently keep your champion near the border of your game once you can do this practice moving the top lane from your base with manual mouse panning nudge your camera so your champion is at the bottom edge then move your mouse back to around the halfway point between the middle of your screen and the top edge spam right clicks until your champion gets close back to the center of your camera and then you nudge the camera again and repeat this process the key to this is never panning your camera so far that your camera up spam right click then left spam right click and repeat you then want to go back the other way down the river to practice panning your camera down nudge your camera down send movement titre: WASD Movement Explained - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: This video will go over the new WD movement in League of Legends, which has just hit the PBE. When you load into a game, you have the choice to use these WD controls instead. This is where your entire character's movement is now used by WD. And all your abilities go to right mouse click, shift Q, and E. However, these can all be rebinded, and I quite like just moving my Q back to Q, E back to E, alt back to R, and W to right mouse click. But as W is the only one you've actually lost access to, you can just move that to anything. So, as everyone's worked out already, ADCs are the ones who benefit most from this change. This change essentially allows you to perfectly kite and perfectly chase down. You'll no longer be misclicking your movement, and you now don't have to drag your mouse between walking behind you or in front of you while also clicking the enemy. Early game, this isn't that important. Your attack speed is so low that it's not that difficult to click your enemy, then move to where you want to, and then click them again. But every bit of attack speed your character gains shortens that window you have to do everything. So, it's when ADCs are hitting late game team fights this becomes particularly amazing. Being able to perfectly spam out every single bit of damage you physically can while also getting the maximum movement you physically can is going to be very strong. I can almost see a world in which people use the normal movement for most of the game and then when you have enough attack speed means you change to this movement. So with ranged auto attackers, if it stays how it is, it's definitely going to be viable. With other roles, it feels a bit stranger with melee champions. It definitely feels a bit clunky where if you go to attack someone but you're not quite in range, your character won't move towards them if you interrupt it with your WD movement. and it becomes particularly weird for roles like jungle. The map in League of Legends doesn't have your opponent's base left or right or up or down. It has it in the top right or bottom left. This means when moving between your camps, you kind of have to diagonally move through the jungle. And because of this jankier movement, I was finding I was losing time on my jungle clears. Next, one of the biggest changes that comes with this is you more or less have to use locked camera. Most people who watch my videos know I always advocate for getting off locked camera as fast as you can. But with these controls, if you move your camera away from your character, you now have to perfectly move your character into the new area, where before you can just click in the new area, knowing your character will 100% walk to there. So, as a good middle ground, they've introduced dynamic camera. This is where it always keeps your character in the screen, but also allows you to see a lot more of the map than fixed camera. Honestly, this will be a great tool for new players and is a good way to address the issues. The other thing that's important to mention, of course, is that it is on the PVE, which means it's not hitting live. The Balance team do seem super aware that this could be incredibly overpowered. I'm sure they don't want a situation in which you have to use this, and they especially won't want it to be meta where you have to change the controls midway through the game, otherwise you're not getting perfect kiting. It's going to stay on the PvE for a few patches before moving to normals, then moving to ranked, which hopefully gives them enough time to make it viable, but not brokenly overpowered. So, my thoughts on all this. These changes have been brought in to help new players pick up the game with controls that are more familiar to them. I think this is a very good thing. League of Legends is just a very difficult game to pick up. My entire channel is built around helping people get better and more knowledgeable about the game. And it definitely seems that RTO know that it's difficult to get into the game and are making active steps to help. And because they seem aware that it could be a massive balance issue, I'm quietly hopeful.

titre: What The League Of Legends Tutorial DOESN'T Tell You - Beginner Guide
transcription: everyone and welcome to another video and today I'm doing a very special video I Feel over the course of my channel I've kind of neglected the lower ELO Community I'm talking below bronze like iron even you know bronze maybe low silver but even talking level 30s pre-level 30s and I feel like these players the players that really maybe are scared to get into ranked or are maybe dissuaded from playing ranked I believe These Guys these people are the future of the game and so I felt inspired to make a video to what you know directed at these play these players the players that are new to the game who want to get into ranked maybe a little bit anxious and really want to get off on the right foot and so for those of you who are more familiar with my content I would highly suggest you send this to people who are looking to get into the game maybe your brother your father your uncle or a Frank colleague at work or whatever it might be someone who wants to get started gets set off on the right foot this is the video for them okay so we're gonna dive in and I'm really gonna try and cover everything you really need to know to get started League will be the hardest game you play by far most of your friends and now I'm talking to you new players right now most of your friends who already play the game will not appreciate how hard the game is they will completely and utterly underestimate how much about the game they actually know you should actually ask them as a little bit of an experiment the following questions how many hours have you played League Bot games normal games a Rams ranked all together roughly what was your MOBA experience like or gaming background like before heading into League were you an FPS gamer you play Starcraft like horn DOTA what did you play what season did you learn the game did you did that did that friend have the luxury of learning the game in an earlier season like season five season six season four season three whatever it might be because the earlier you learned League of Legends the more forgiving the game was now the game is a lot more sophisticated these questions will give you at least a basic insight into their league journey and why they might be having an easier time with the game than you might be understanding your lead journey is something people fundamentally fail to do let's compare two people's League Journey let's take Bob long term PC Gamer played Starcraft DOTA both at a very high level was highly competitive at every single PC game he played when he played League maybe he also had the luxury of picking up League during maybe a holiday period at schools we had a lot of time to sink into the game if we compare that that Journey with with the with Jim for example let's say Jim was actually a console gamer he played a lot of PlayStation growing up maybe his friends didn't play PC his family didn't accept PC gaming whatever it might be and maybe when he picked up League he was picking it picking it up while he was working full-time and didn't really have that much time to put into the game we can tell with these two Journeys that they're gonna have vastly different experiences with the game so out of these two Journeys who do you think had the easier time picking up League pretty obviously Bob was going to have a much easier time so this is why we fundamentally cannot compare the league journeys of any player because the context in which we learn the game is very important if you want to learn more about this this whole concept of understanding your lead journey I have a whole video on my YouTube channel dedicated towards it uh check it out I'll put the link in the description one of the things that pisses me off the most is that people really really fail to understand how hard the game is people who even now casually play the game they look past the hundreds and thousands of hours they put in and will say things to you when you're playing with them just go in and kill him why don't you just go in and kill him come on it's so obvious a single trade in Lane requires many many things that are just a lot of people are blind to because it's all muscle memory it's all intuition for example Mastery over my range assessment and my damage assessment uh good quality camera control ability to click and move accurately knowledge over what the enemy kit does his ranges his damage his cooldowns awareness of the minions and how the minions are interacting in the way State the list goes on a lot of this for players who have played the game for quite a long time this is all muscle memory they don't need to think or use their their prefrontal courses to think about these things okay very important that we understand what's going on here so what would I recommend to someone who's starting out at the game brand new what would I say well first things first you need to play as many champions as possible across all roles have fun or only play normals in Bot games don't rush into ranked during this time you don't want to be thinking too much you don't really want to be ingesting too much content you just want to play fill out the game have a lot of fun now I have a very unique opinion about board games I think Bot games are severely underutilized I think you should start with Bots and you should be basically farming Bots before you move on to ranked Bots should be boring before you go into normal games so you should be killing Bots 12 15 times in a game before you move on because what Bot games do is it really allows you to gain a lot of Mastery over your Champion without overwhelming your mental stack and the concept of a mental stack we'll touch on later on we'll kind of cover that concept later but I just don't feel like people rush into rank for no real good reason your goal in these normal games when you do eventually get to normal games is to gain a solid understanding of what every champion in the game does and I'll only move on to ranked when I had solid Mastery on my champion and I had a solid understanding of at least at a baseline level what every champion in the game does and a lot of people skip over this they get interranked and they try to learn the game in rank so you're actually simultaneously doing two things if you rush into ranked your you're trying to compete and you're trying to learn but when you're playing in normal games and Bot games you can purely focus on learning without the pressure of losing the game uh for your teammates or losing LP whatever it might be now some people like myself will really struggle with the mechanical aspect of the game when I first came to League of Legends I literally had to look at the keyboard I I couldn't place my my fingers on the qwe I'm like like I'm trying to show you what I would do I would look at the screen the monitor try and control my character and then I'd have to direct my eyes down to to my keyboard to make sure that my fingers were placed in the correct place on the keyboard because I came from an FPS background so I was used to having wasd you know having my pinky on shift and like moving my my forefinger from D to F things like that I've never used qwer I didn't come from a sophisticated uh mobile background or even like a uh like a World of Warcraft background or anything like that so it took me a very long time it literally like weeks and months to stop looking at my keyboard and so purely focus on my screen and even though that's that makes that may sound ridiculous to some people you've got to remember this ties back to understanding your league Journey for some people it will take a year literally a year just to feel comfortable with controlling your character and I'm not here to judge anyone and this is the point of this video everyone is at different stages of their Learning Journey and because of the context of the of your gaming background everyone picks up the game differently something that I'm really passionate about is that um for a lot of people who neutral PC game so let's say you came through the route of a console gamer let's say you'd only played Xbox or Playstation or Nintendo or whatever it might be when you first play league say league is your first PC game you're actually doing two things you're simultaneously learning the game of League Legends but you're also learning to play an online multiplayer PC game you're doing two things at once but for an old school PC Gamer they are purely playing and learning League of Legends they don't need to know what it feels like to hold a mouse and keyboard and have comfortability in an online environment on a PC so it's you know it again it sounds this is something we Overlook a lot within the league Community now there are a series of underlying game fundamentals that I think are very helpful to know and these are things that I wish I knew when I was starting out playing League of Legends Cs and farm is extremely important roughly 15 CS is worth about a kill's worth of gold and gold at XP dictates the strength of a given Champion the more golden XP the better and when you're starting to you know when you're playing and starting the game for the first time you need to feel what it's like to get to your key item spikes you won't know what you're missing out on until this moment and you may ask what So, typically when you first create your League of Legends account or Riot account and you first boot up League of Legends, they will require you to play the game in tutorial mode first. But typically in tutorial mode, you should see a bottom icon here at the bottom right that says leave. So, you can go and just click on it. It's going to say, are you sure you want to leave the tutorial? If you do, you can come back anytime before level 11 and claim your reward. So, you go and just click on leave here, and this will redirect you in this page here. Now, obviously, in order for you to start playing other modes, you need to complete the beginner intermediate modes here first until you're level 11. Well, it means that League of Legends is actually uh lagging or um is stuck on that specific page or in the tutorial page. Now, typically to solve that, so if you're having that problem, well, what you need to do is you want to go and just press control shift escape on your keyboard. And this should open up task manager. Now, if you don't want to press that, you could go to your search bar here. Same thing. Just type in task manager. This should open it up. titre: How to play Riftbound TCG in 3 minutes! - League of Legends TCG
transcription: how to play project K in under 3 minutes step one acquire two decks this might be hard considering that these decks are not available and easily accessible step two set up the board each deck has a legend and a champion card which are available from the start of the game next you want to filter out the Rune cards which have a white background and place them in the Rune pile you'll want to then look for cards with black backs which are your Battlefield cards and one from each player will be placed on the battlefield next you'll want to shuffle your deck of blue cards and these will be placed on the deck slot the way the battlefields are chosen will depend on the format and the number of players you are playing with place a coin to track score for both players step three start playing the game both players draw four cards the player that goes first can be determined by shuffling the battlefields and selecting one at random during the Mulligan phase both players can choose two cards to move to the bottom of the deck and then draw that many at the start of each player's turn you may draw one card then draw two ready runes from the Rune pile the last player plays an extra Rune on the first turn runes allow you to play different cards including units gear which can be placed on units as well as spells which can be used during combat your turn or as a reaction to play a card you must exhaust the number of runes on the top left the color of your runes does not matter for playing cards but for recycling some cards will recycle runes which move the runes to the bottom of the Rune pile recycling can be done on exhausted runes during your turn if you have the resources you can play the champion card from the champion Zone and also trigger the ability of your Legend any cards that have been used this turn will be ex exhausted at the start of your turn all exhausted cards become ready and can be used unit cards can be played from the hand to the base when units enter the field they are played in the exhausted State and cannot take any actions an active unit can move from your base to a battlefield if there are enemy units on the battlefield you start a combat during the combat phase each player sums up the might value at the top right and then divides that as total damage to the opposing units each unit has Health which is equivalent to the amount of its might and at the end of the combat phase units that don't die return to full health if the attackers live take control of the battlefield and score a point if the Defenders live The Defenders then stay and the attackers are either killed or returned to base units can only move from Battlefield to your base unless they have the effect ganking to move a unit from a battlefield to another Battlefield you must move it to your base first then from your base to the Target Battlefield when starting a new turn if you have units controlling a battlefield you get one point to win the game a player must score eight points the final Point can only only be earned by conquering both battlefields or by holding a battlefield until the player's next turn if at seven points and you conquer a battlefield and you have not taken control of the other battlefields you get to draw one card instead of gaining a point and that's pretty much it almost all you need to know to play project K in under three minutes

titre: 3 Minute Graves Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: Why play Graves jungle? Well, he brings massive burst damage. He's great early, mid, and late game. His clear is brilliant and fast, and he's a very mobile champion. However, he is very dependent on skill shots, and he is damage reliant, so can struggle from behind. Graves' passive is his shotgun. His basic attacks deal damage in a cone where he shoots out four pellets from his shotgun. These deal damage to enemies hit, and if you hit the same target with multiple pellets, they take more damage. So, the closer you stand to the target, usually the more damage. However, after he shoots twice, he has to reload. Also, non- champions hit by his attacks are knocked back, making his clear very healthy. Graves' Q is a straight line skill shot, which deals damage to enemies hit, and it leaves a gunpowder trail on the floor. After 2 seconds, or if the ability was hit into terrain, the gunpowder explodes, dealing damage to enemies within. Graves' W is one of the most underrated abilities in the game. It throws out a circle onto the floor, which deals damage to enemies within, slows them, and causes them to become nearsighted. Graves's E is an auto attack reset which causes him to dash in a direction and reload one shell. When he does this, he gains a stack which grants him bonus armor. And if he dashes towards an enemy champion, he gains two stacks. The cooldown of this ability is reduced every time he lands one of his shotgun pellets onto an enemy. Graves's ultimate is a longrange straightline skill shot, which deals damage to all enemies hit. Upon reaching max range or hitting an enemy champion, it then explodes into a cone which deals slightly reduced damage. For combos, start with W into an attack. Follow up with E into an attack and then Q and alt before attacking away. However, a combo you'll often have to use to engage is to E into W. From here, attack into Q before attacking into alt. For matchups, Graves is great into squishier AD junglers who can easily explode while tanking their damage, and he can struggle against some of the tankier champions who will simply lock him down. For runes, take this. Dark Harvest makes your burst even scarier. For build order, start red monster and pot into a ghost blade and tier 2 boots. From here, a collector and lord doms are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed. For skill order, start E, then Q, then W before maxing Q, then E, then W, taking alt whenever you can. For summoner spells, take flash and smite. Starting the game, you almost always want to do a full jungle clear. Between your attacks, knocking the monsters away, and the extra armor you get from your E, you clearly healthily. This means you'll always be in a good position to contest Scuttle Crab. And if an enemy jungler ever tries to fight you for it, as long as you land your Q and W, you just simply deal too much damage. From here, look to gank. As always, consider the three main points of where is lane push to, what escapes does the enemy have, and what CC does your team bring. Your ganks are fairly strong. Where your W isn't hard CC, it may as well be for how confusing it is to play against. You easily get onto them with E, and if you can land both parts of your Q, they'll simply disappear from the map. Entering the mid game, you're simply looking to play a pick and objective play style. Collapse onto a lane, explode someone off the map, and then take the objective nearby. Entering late game team fights, you want to put out constant damage while waiting for your chance to explode a carry. You don't have the range to be a backline carry, so position well on the edge of fights, constantly avoiding any skill shots while spamming out damage. And that's how to play Graves jungle. If this video helped you, leave a like and comment below who you want to see

titre: How to Play on PBE - League of Legends | League of Legends 2025
transcription: In today's video, I'm going to teach you how to play on PBE League of Legends. The public beta environment or PBE is a separate test server where upcoming features, champions, and balance changes go first. Before we begin, there's one crucial requirement. You need a PBE account granted by Riot. Without that entitlement, you won't be able to access the PBE option or log in, and that's expected behavior. This walkthrough is for those who already have access and want to launch the PBE from the Riot client. Start by opening your Riot client and signing in. On the left side of the client, click the League of Legends icon to open the League game page. Now, look closely at the big play button on the right. Next to it, you'll see a small arrow or a version selector. Click that arrow. A list will appear, and among the options, you should see League of Legends PBE. Click it to switch your client to the PBE version. If your account has PBE access, the client will prompt you to install or update the PBE build. This is separate from your Live League installation, so expect a full download. Let the installation run. Don't worry if it takes a while as PBE patches can be sizable and sometimes ahead of live. Keep the client open and let it finish. Once the install completes, the play button will reflect the PBE selection and you're ready to launch. If you don't have a PBE account, you won't be able to proceed past this point. The PBE option may be grayed out, hidden, or it will ask you to sign in with an eligible account. That's normal. PBE access is controlled by Riot and can't be bypassed from the client. A few quick tips to make things smooth. Make sure you're signed into the exact Riot account that has PBE access. If you recently received access, restarting the client can refresh the available options. Confirm you have enough disk space since the PBE is a full game installation. Be patient with downloads and Q times. PBE servers can be busier and are designed for testing, so performance may vary compared to live. Once everything is installed, click play. If prompted, sign in again and you'll land in the PBE version of the League client. From here, you can cue and play as usual, keeping in mind that content here is experimental and may change before release. To recap, you need PBE access from Riot. Then in the Riot client, select the League icon, click the arrow next to play, choose League of Legends PBE, and install when prompted. That's all you need to start playing on the PBE.

titre: 3 Minute Kassadin Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: why play cadin mid well he is a late game God he's good against magic damage he has insane mobility and he can Solo Carry games with ease however he does have a much weaker early game and he lacks having CC heavily relying on damage Caden's passive is that he's permanently ghosted and takes 10% reduced magic damage cadin C is a pointand click ability which deals damage to the Target hit and disrupts ongoing channels which work on abilities like Katarina's Al and garen's spin in addition he also gains a magic damage Shield Caden's W has a passive where his basic attacks deal a large amount bonus magic damage on hit the active is an auto attack reset which makes his next attack have bonus range deal bonus damage and restore mana and he gets five times the Mana restore if used against Champions cadin e has a passive where when a friendly or enemy ability is used near him the cool down of this is reduced the active is a cone skill shot which deals damage to All Enemies hit and slows them cen's ult causes him to Blink in a direction dealing damage to all enemies nearby this ability has a very low cool down starting off at only 5 seconds and going all the way down to 2 seconds when maxed for each use of this ability cadin also gains a stack for 15 seconds each stack increases the damage of this ability but massively increases the Mana cost and this can be stacked up to four times for combos use ult to jump onto your opponent into your e attack into your W Auto attack reset into your que before keeping on jumping on your opponent spamming your attacks and abilities for matchups Caston is great into AP midlaners who he naturally has a lot of built-in protection against and they won't be able to stop him from scaling and he can struggle against Champions that can bully him early and slow down his scaling for runes take this first strike is great extra damage and money for build order start shield and pot into a malignance and tier 2 boots after this an archangel and z are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed for skill ORD start Q then W then E before maxing e then W then Q taking alt whenever you can for summon of spells take Flash and teleport starting the game your main focus is going to be on scaling into a late game God and the best way to do this is by farming heart and taking good trades with your opponent against magic damage users Caden's trades aren't that bad starting with Q will give you the magic damage Shield following up with an auto attack into your W is a great amount of Mana back as well as great damage and your ear is great damage and slows them to stop them from coming back at you which overall isn't a bad trade especially if you get to proc First Strike but when in doubt simply let your opponent push you in and farm under Tower when you hit Level six you are now much safer in lane and can look to roam especially using ultimate and E to push a wave in means you can easily roam top or bot entering the mid game your Bot Lane will come mid which puts your gold and experience in a side lane Cid in is very happy split pushing at this point in the game his ultimate and E clears waves quickly and easily meaning he can start scaling incredibly quickly and if anyone comes to stop him it's almost impossible to chase a ctin over so many walls entering late gain team fights this is where cadin truly shines at this point in the game your full combo will more or less one shot an enemy carry so flank onto a team fight jump onto the enemy carry and blow them up use Zas for safety after or simply jump onto your next victim and that's how to play cassid in mid if this video helped you leave it a like and comment below who you want to see next to

titre: How to Play Riftbound in 11 Minutes! | League of Legends TCG
transcription: Whether you're a seasoned jungler, a casual arcane enthusiast, a diehard KDA stand, or you don't know the first thing about League, Riftbound, the League of Legends trading card game might be right up your alley, or should I say right up your lane. It's a League of Legends term. In Riftbound from Riot Games, players take on the role of a legend, commanding units in battle, using gear for advantages, slinging powerful spells for powerful effects, all in the hunt to conquer and hold battlefields to score eight points and win. There are multiple formats, including one-on-one matches, four on four free-for-alls, twoon two team games, but for this video, we'll focus on one-on-one play. Setup. To play a game of Riftbound, players assemble two decks. A main deck of the cards they'll play in battle and a separate rune deck that serves as the primary resources to pay for those main deck cards. They'll also bring three synergistic battlefields which provide the primary scoring mechanism for the game. Each deck is built around a champion legend, a character that provides a unique ability that is always active and in play. In game rules, a legend isn't considered a card and can't be sent to the trash or to hand. It's always on board in the legend zone. A legend also determines the domain identity of the main deck. That is which color cards are available in the deck building process. The six domains are fury, body, chaos, calm, mind, and order. In set one called Origins, every champion legend has two domains. Players will construct a 12 card rune deck made up of the two types of runes of their legends domain and a 40card main deck, which also includes a chosen champion. A chosen champion unit is a card with the same tag or character name as the player's legend. For example, Jinx Rebel can be paired with Jinx Loose Cannon, classic double Jinx. This chosen champion unit card starts on the table in the champion zone next to the rest of the main deck and is always available to play if a player has enough runes to pay for it. Notably, once the chosen champion dies, it goes to the trash like any other card. A main deck can have up to three copies of the same named card, including that chosen champion, and three total cards with the signature spell tag that also match the tag of the champion legend. Jinx, for example, has Super Mega Death Rocket as a signature spell with Jinx as a tag. Because if you wanted a subtle weapon, you probably wouldn't have decided to be a Jinx player. Anatomy of a card. There are three types of cards in a main deck. Spells, units, and gear. So, we're going to break down each of them. Spells are a one-time use effect that go to the trash after being played. Get excited is a spell. It has an energy and power cost, a keyword that determines its timing, and an effect. Its cost can be paid by exhausting two runes of any color, and generating one fury power, either by recycling a fury rune by returning it to the bottom of the rune deck, or by using another power generating source, like a seal of rage. Little strategy tip, you can use a rune to pay for the spell's energy cost first and then recycle it while exhausted to pay for the power cost. Units, they're just little guys. They'll usually hang out in a player's base or in their personal play area until the player decides to send them into combat. Check out Master Yi Meditative. He costs five energy and one calm power. He has tags which give more information about the card and a might, his combat statistic in the top right corner. Might represents both the unit's damage dealt and the amount it can withstand before being killed and sent to the trash. A main deck can also include gear cards. These are ongoing tools you'll be using during gameplay. Forge of the future is a gear that costs two energy and has both a when played effect that makes a recruit token and an ongoing ability that allows you to kill it to recycle up to four cards from the trash. Gear enters ready and players can exhaust them to use their abilities immediately. Battlefields. Now that players have their main decks and rune decks ready to go, it's time to choose battlefields. Players will bring three options for their battlefield that synergize with their deck. For example, since Jinx here likes to discard, Zhan Warren would be a good choice for her battlefield. But you know who I would never discard? Jinx. I can't fix her, but she can make me worse. Pat Benitar was right. Love is a battlefield. Though each player brings three battlefields to the match, they'll select just one so that each player contributes one battlefield for a total of two. In a best of one game, players will randomly pick one of their three battlefields. In a best of three match, they'll get to choose which battlefield they want to use, but must select a different battlefield for each subsequent game of the match. What does a battlefield do, you ask? During game play, players score points by moving units to battlefields to conquer them, and then score additional points by holding, which means starting their turn controlling a battlefield. Unlike combat aids in which players reduce their opponent's life total, Riftbound scoring mechanism is constructive as players race to be the first to score eight points. So set those point markers to zero and it's time to play. Game play. Players will each shuffle their main decks and rune decks. Place their champion legend and chosen champion on the board and select a battlefield. Then determine a first player. We like to shuffle the battlefields face down and select one at random. That battlefield's owner gets to choose who goes first. Then players draw a starting hand of four cards. They now have an opportunity to mulligan once by recycling up to two cards maximum from their starting hand, placing them on the bottom of the deck and redrawing to replace them. The first player begins with their start of turn and the handy acronym A B C D. Awaken ready all exhausted cards. B, beginning check for beginning of turn effects, most importantly holding a battlefield or an ability on a card like Jinx Loose Cannon. C, channel the top two runes from the rune deck. The second player in a two-player match gets to channel three runes on their first turn. And D, draw a card. That one easy. Next up, in the action phase, the active player can play any cards in their hand that they have the runes to pay for. cards might have timing keywords such as action or reaction. Action cards can be played on the active player's turn or during showdowns. I'll cover what a showdown is in just a moment. When a card is played, it's considered on the chain. Cards with reaction can be played even before other spells and abilities on the chain resolve. However, reactions require a trigger before they can be played, such as a spell being played, an ability being activated, a card being chosen, or a showdown being initiated. When no other cards are able to be played in reaction, then the chain resolves. There are no reaction triggers when playing a unit or gear. So, when you play a unit, it's guaranteed to make it to the base. If the card has neither the action or reaction keyword, a player may only play them on their turn when no other cards are waiting to be resolved. Units can be played to either a player's base or to a battlefield they control, which is one they already have a unit on. Units typically enter play exhausted, but they might have an ability keyword like accelerate that allows them to enter ready. So, you've assembled an army and gear, you have powerful spells at the ready. It's time to talk about conquering and combat. The juice of scoring points in Riftbound. Conquering and combat. A player may exhaust a unit to move it, either from a base to a battlefield, retreating from battlefield to base, or if the unit has the super awesome keyword ganking, move from battlefield to battlefield. Players can move one unit or multiple at the same time. When a player moves units to a battlefield they don't control, it starts a showdown. Moving to an unoccupied battlefield still starts a showdown. If a player moves units to a battlefield that isn't controlled, an open showdown begins. The attacking player starts with a window to play actions, then their opponent can play actions. Reactions can also be played at any time during showdowns. After any actions and reactions resolve, if the attacking unit is still alive on the battlefield, that player scores a point by conquering. and now is in control of that battlefield. They now have the option to play future units directly to that battlefield instead of to their base. If they move one or more units to a battlefield that has an opponent's unit on it, a combat showdown occurs. The total might scores of all the attacking units are compared to the total might scores of all the defending units. Any actions and reactions played are then resolved, whether they remove a unit, change might scores, or more. and then the damage is dealt. Each player assigns damage to their opponent's units in the order of their choice, but they must assign the exact amount of damage needed to kill a unit. No partial damage and no overkill. Then, if there are any attacking units remaining, they've conquered that battlefield and earn the active player one point. If at the start of their next turn, during the beginning phase, they still control that battlefield with a unit, they score another point for holding. I also want to call out that damage is healed on all units as soon as combat resolves. So, if you're planning on multiple combats at the same battlefield in one turn, just know that your opponent's units might will reset between each combat. There's no wearing them down. Winning the game. The first player to score eight points wins the game. But to score your final point, you must either hold a battlefield or score with both battlefields in the same turn. That means you can win by holding for your eighth point, holding for your seventh and conquering for the eighth, or conquering for seven and then conquering for eight on the same turn. If you have seven points and conquer without yet having scored the other battlefield though, instead of getting a point, you draw a card, which is also sweet. And that's the basics of Riftbound. There is so much strategy in this game, from deck building to timing to the choreography of movement into and out of battlefields that I hope you'll discover as you dig deeper. As you play, you'll find even more keywords and abilities like assault, shield, and hidden. If you want a more in-depth look at the game, check out Riot's official tutorial linked in the description below, and perhaps you'll feel comforted by a familiar face. It's me. Thanks for watching, and I'll see you in the rift because I live there now. Jinx is my girlfriend. We've moved really quickly, but I'm feeling good about it. If you want to send us a housewarming gift, check out the link to our Patreon below and consider becoming one of our little freaks. Little freaks get exclusive videos, behind the scenes peaks, and you'll be making Jinx My House a home. And once you've signed up, come on back here for more great games, and good times on Good Time Society. Bye-bye.
